question: Define a `Book` class with private data members `title` (string), `author` (string), and `isbn` (string). Provide public getter methods `getTitle()`, `getAuthor()`, `getIsbn()`, and a `displayBookInfo()` method that prints all book details.

test_cases.input: Book("The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy", "Douglas Adams", "978-0345391803")
test_cases.expected:
--- Book 1 ---
Title: The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy
Author: Douglas Adams
ISBN: 978-0345391803
--- End Book 1 ---
--- Book 2 ---
Title: 1984
Author: George Orwell
ISBN: 978-0451524935
--- End Book 2 ---
--- Book 3 ---
Title: 
Author: Unknown Author
ISBN: 
--- End Book 3 ---

errors: The use of `using namespace std;` in the global scope can lead to name collisions and reduced readability, especially in larger projects. It's generally better practice to qualify `std` members or use specific `using` declarations within functions/classes.

The getter methods (`getTitle`, `getAuthor`, `getIsbn`) return `std::string` by value. This creates unnecessary copies of the string objects, which is inefficient. They should ideally return `const std::string&` to avoid copies.

The getter methods (`getTitle`, `getAuthor`, `getIsbn`) and `displayBookInfo` are not marked `const`. These methods do not modify the object's state and should be `const` to allow them to be called on `const` objects and improve code correctness and clarity.

The `displayBookInfo` method uses `std::endl` on each line, which flushes the output buffer every time. While not incorrect, using `\n` characters and flushing less frequently (e.g., once at the end of the method or letting `std::cout` buffer automatically) can be more efficient, especially for multiple output operations.
